summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes Stacked LoRA, an adaptation framework designed to mitigate inter-subject variability when fine-tuning frozen EEG foundation models (REVE, LaBraM, and LUNA). The method structurally separates the low-rank update into a shared Global adapter for subject-invariant task knowledge and individual Subject-Specific adapters to absorb personal neural signatures. Experiments on three motor imagery datasets demonstrate that this decoupled approach generally outperforms single-path adaptation, with the optimal balance shifting toward global paths for large cohorts and subject-specific paths for high-variability clinical data.

headline_results: Stacked LoRA achieved best or tied-best balanced accuracy in 7 out of 9 backbone-dataset combinations.

key_points: Method for EEG-FM adaptation: Introduces Stacked LoRA to decouple subject-invariant and subject-specific knowledge during fine-tuning.
Versatile performance: Outperforms linear probing and single-path LoRA across REVE, LaBraM, and LUNA backbones on multiple MI datasets.
Population-dependent utility: Demonstrates that shared adapters suffice for large, diverse cohorts, while subject-specific paths are critical for high-variability clinical recordings.
limitations: Requires subject identity at inference time, preventing zero-shot personalization for unseen subjects.
Training cost increases linearly with the number of subjects due to joint optimization of all subject-specific adapters.
Subject-specific adapters offer diminishing returns when data per subject is very scarce.
Evaluation was restricted to motor imagery decoding with within-dataset splits.

method.architecture: Frozen backbone (REVE, LaBraM, LUNA) + Stacked LoRA (Global adapter r=32, Subject-Specific adapters r=8) + linear classification head.
method.finetuning: Joint optimization of Global and Subject-Specific LoRA weights using AdamW with cosine-annealing.
method.objective: Cross-entropy loss for motor imagery classification.
method.pretraining: Utilizes existing pretrained weights from REVE, LaBraM, and LUNA.

summary.one_liner: A dual-path LoRA framework that decouples global task knowledge from subject-specific signatures to improve the adaptation of EEG foundation models for motor imagery decoding.
summary.title: Stacked LoRA for Subject-Adaptive EEG Foundation Models in Motor Imagery Decoding
summary.unique_contribution: Proposes a dual-path adaptation architecture (Stacked LoRA) that explicitly decouples population-level task knowledge from individual subject variability during the fine-tuning of frozen EEG foundation models.

papers.title: Stacked LoRA for Subject-Adaptive EEG Foundation Models in Motor Imagery Decoding

reasons: Explicitly targets the adaptation and fine-tuning of existing EEG foundation models such as REVE, LaBraM, and LUNA.
Proposes a specific framework (Stacked LoRA) to decouple subject-invariant and subject-specific knowledge within EEG-FMs.
Focuses on the EEG-FM ecosystem by addressing the challenge of adapting large pretrained EEG models to downstream tasks.

papers.title: Omni-Sleep: A Sleep Foundation Model via Hierarchical Contrastive Learning of CNS-ANS Dynamics

reasons: Explicitly identifies as a 'sleep foundation model' (Omni-Sleep)
Pre-trained on a massive scale of over 100,000 hours of multimodal PSG data including EEG

EEG is a central modality used to capture CNS dynamics within the foundation model framework

summary.title: NeuroOnline: Bridging Pretraining and Online Adaptation for EEG Foundation Models

papers.title: NeuroOnline: Bridging Pretraining and Online Adaptation for EEG Foundation Models

reasons: Explicitly targets EEG foundation models in both the title and abstract.
Proposes a framework for online adaptation and alignment of pretrained EEG representations.
Addresses the gap between pretraining and downstream deployment for EEG-FMs.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes STST-JEPA, a transformer-based foundation model that combines a latent-prediction objective (predicting masked tokens against an EMA-of-tokenizer target) with an auxiliary signal-reconstruction term. It introduces a Pooled Multihead Attention (PMA) block to handle heterogeneous EEG montages by collapsing the channel axis into a fixed set of tokens. Pretrained on 47,703 sessions, the model achieves state-of-the-art results on the NeuralBench x brain.space leaderboard for sex, age, and psychopathology tasks.

headline_results: Rank 1 in sex, age, and psychopathology on NeuralBench x brain.space
Internal age regression MAE of 3.06 years

key_points: New EEG foundation model: STST-JEPA uses a joint latent-prediction and signal-reconstruction objective to learn representations from 47,703 heterogeneous EEG sessions.
Architecture novelty: Employs a Pooled Multihead Attention (PMA) block and coordinate embeddings to unify different electrode montages into a shared token space.
Strong evidence: Achieves rank-1 performance on the NeuralBench x brain.space leaderboard for sex, age, and psychopathology regression from a single backbone.
limitations: Lack of controlled ablations for the joint objective components.
Internal age regression results reported as best validation rather than fixed protocol test.
Performance on age prediction drops to rank 4 when using standard 2s windows on NeuralBench.
Behavioral correlations are small in magnitude (|r| < 0.10).
No clinical validity claims made for the brain age biomarker.
Potential confounding of BAG by the mixture of resting and task paradigms in pretraining.

method.architecture: 24-layer transformer encoder with a tokenizer using 1D convs and PMA channel pooling.
method.finetuning: Downstream tasks use either a frozen attentive probe or light fine-tuning of final layers.
method.objective: Joint MSE latent prediction (against an EMA tokenizer) and smooth-L1 signal reconstruction.
method.pretraining: Self-supervised pretraining on 47,703 EEG sessions using a shallow-target JEPA framework.

summary.one_liner: A self-supervised EEG foundation model using a joint latent-prediction and signal-reconstruction objective to enable robust brain-age estimation and cross-task transfer.
summary.title: STST-JEPA: Shallow-Target Spatio-Temporal Joint Embedding Prediction Architecture For EEG Self-Supervised Learning
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a joint SSL objective combining shallow-target latent prediction with signal reconstruction, paired with a coordinate-aware PMA block for montage-flexible EEG tokenization.

papers.title: STST-JEPA: Shallow-Target Spatio-Temporal Joint Embedding Prediction Architecture For EEG Self-Supervised Learning

reasons: Explicitly frames the work as addressing the need for EEG foundation models.
Pretrained on a large-scale dataset of 47,703 EEG sessions using a self-supervised transformer architecture.
Demonstrates broad transferability across multiple downstream tasks including age prediction, sex classification, and psychopathology regression.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes CoCoT-EEG, which replaces standard patch-first tokenization with a convolution-first approach using parallel 1D convolutional branches to extract multiscale temporal features. Unlike most EEG foundation models that use masked reconstruction, CoCoT-EEG employs a MoCo-style contrastive pretraining objective to better handle the high noise and low information density of EEG signals. The model was pretrained on the TUEG corpus and demonstrated state-of-the-art performance across 10 diverse decoding tasks, including motor imagery and emotion recognition, outperforming reconstruction-based models on 8 out of 10 benchmarks.

headline_results: Outperformed reconstruction-pretrained models on 8/10 tasks without extra finetuning.
Outperformed reconstruction-pretrained models on 9/10 tasks when using Mixup.

key_points: New EEG foundation model: CoCoT-EEG uses multiscale 1D convolutions for feature extraction before patchification to filter noise and preserve temporal continuity.
Contrastive pretraining: Employs a MoCo-style objective that outperforms masked reconstruction on 8 out of 10 benchmark decoding tasks.
High data efficiency: The base architecture outperforms previous single-task models even when trained from scratch without pretraining.
limitations: Pretraining datasets are smaller than those used by some other recent EEG foundation models.
Augmentation ablations were conducted at a reduced scale.
Reconstruction objectives may still be more appropriate for high-SNR tasks like seizure detection.

method.architecture: Parallel 1D convolutional branches (kernels 3-121), L2 pooling for patchification, Fourier positional encoding, and a Transformer encoder.
method.finetuning: Supervised finetuning using a residual MLP head with GLU blocks.
method.objective: MoCo-style contrastive objective using InfoNCE loss.
method.pretraining: Self-supervised pretraining on TUEG and HBN datasets with EEG-specific augmentations.

summary.one_liner: A contrastive-pretrained EEG foundation model utilizing a multiscale convolutional tokenizer to improve noise robustness and feature extraction.
summary.title: CoCoT-EEG: Contrastive-Pretrained Multiscale Convolutional Transformer for EEG Decoding
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a convolution-first tokenizer and a contrastive pretraining objective as a more robust alternative to the prevailing masked reconstruction paradigm for EEG foundation models.

papers.title: CoCoT-EEG: Contrastive-Pretrained Multiscale Convolutional Transformer for EEG Decoding

reasons: Explicitly frames the work as developing a contrastive-pretrained model for 'building EEG FMs'.
Evaluates the model on 'extensive benchmark decoding tasks with heterogeneous electrode configurations', demonstrating broad transferability.
Directly compares the proposed approach against other 'reconstruction-pretrained EEG models' within the foundation model paradigm.

summary.detailed_summary: DiffEEG proposes a 1D U-Net based foundation model pre-trained on 1.3 million unlabeled EEG segments using a denoising diffusion objective. It introduces a Reinforced Decision Layer (RDL) that uses policy gradient optimization to directly maximize the F1-score, addressing extreme class imbalance in clinical seizure data. Evaluation on the TUHSZ corpus shows 81% accuracy for binary detection and 61% accuracy for 4-class seizure subtyping under strict patient-wise splits.

headline_results: Binary Detection: 81% accuracy, 85% weighted F1, 0.7557 AUROC

key_points: New EEG foundation model: DiffEEG uses a 1D U-Net with multi-head self-attention and FiLM conditioning, pre-trained via a denoising diffusion objective.
Introduces a Reinforced Decision Layer (RDL) using policy gradient optimization to prioritize sensitivity to rare seizure events over global accuracy.
Achieves 81% accuracy in binary seizure detection and 0.922 AUROC on the TUAB abnormality detection benchmark.
limitations: Difficulty in cross-patient generalization due to neurophysiological diversity
Sensitivity to variations in recording equipment
Need for validation across more diverse datasets to ensure clinical reliability

method.architecture: 1D U-Net with multi-head self-attention, FiLM conditioning, and sinusoidal position embeddings
method.finetuning: Reinforced Decision Layer (RDL) using policy gradient (REINFORCE) to maximize F1-score
method.objective: Denoising diffusion (MSE between added and predicted noise)
method.pretraining: Self-supervised pre-training on 1.3 million unlabeled EEG segments from the TUHSZ corpus

summary.one_liner: A self-supervised EEG foundation model using denoising diffusion pre-training and reinforcement learning for imbalanced seizure detection.
summary.title: DiffEEG: A Self-Supervised Denoising Diffusion Model for Learning EEG Generic Representations
summary.unique_contribution: Combines denoising diffusion pre-training with a reinforcement learning-based decision layer to optimize for F1-score in highly imbalanced EEG seizure datasets.

papers.title: DiffEEG: A Self-Supervised Denoising Diffusion Model for Learning EEG Generic Representations

reasons: Explicitly describes DiffEEG as a 'self-supervised foundation model'
Pre-trained on a large-scale unlabeled dataset (1.3M segments) to learn 'generic neural representations'
Demonstrates downstream adaptation for seizure detection and subtyping tasks
